metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Path-Aware Semantic Addressing (PASA) for Low power and Lossy Networks
Generic Address Assignment Option for 6LoWPAN Neighbor Discovery
Transmission of SCHC-compressed packets over IEEE 802.15.4 networks
Transmission of IPv6 Packets over SR OWCs

transcript.text: Thank you. Thank you. Okay, so it seems like it's the time to get started. So, hello, everyone. Welcome to the meeting of the Six Law Working Group. Please make sure that you are in the right room. My name is Carlos Gomez. The other chair is Shweta Vandari. As you can see, we are both remote today. And our in-person delegate is Luigi Iannone. Thank you very much, Luigi, for helping. And our responsible AD, is Eric Vinque, also on site. And we would need to have someone help us, a minute taker. Is there anyone who would like to volunteer? I'm here, so when I'm not presenting, I can help. Okay, thank you very much, Luigi. That would be very helpful. So, yeah, let's hope that we can also cover when you are presenting. And anyway, recall that we are using HedgeDoc, as usual, as the tool for taking minutes. So, everyone feel free to join and contribute to the process of collaboratively taking minutes as well. Okay, so there are a few tips for this meeting. For in-person participants, please make sure that you sign into the session by using MeetEcho. You may, for instance, use the on-site tool, which is available from the data tracker agenda. And recall that you need to use MeetEcho to join the queue, because there's a single unified queue, which is handled directly from MeetEcho. And also, blue sheets are automatically generated from MeetEcho as well. So, if you are in the physical room and haven't yet joined the MeetEcho session, please do so. And for remote participants, just remember to keep your audio and video off, unless you are presenting. Okay, so this is the Notewell. Recall that by participating in the ITF, you agree to follow ITF processes and policies. And the Notewell is a reminder on some of those policies in topics such as the expected behavior for ITF participants, which should be respectful and professional. Also, on intellectual property rights. Also, on different kinds of procedures, process in the ITF. And if you haven't read yet the Notewell, please take your time to do so. There is a link on the second line of the slide. And also, there's the QR code, which you may capture to access the whole content of the Notewell. So, I'll keep this slide open.  I'll wait for a few seconds, just in case. Okay, so this is the proposed agenda, proposed for today. And the first presentation would be the usual chairs introduction, which is currently in progress. This will be followed by a presentation by Luigi on the status of PASA and GAL. Then, I will present an update of the draft on transmission of sheet compress package of 15.4 networks. And after that, Yang Wang will present the transmission of IPv6 package of a short-range optical wireless communication. And finally, Kerry will present the RFC 8163 update. This will lead to a total of 50 minutes of allocated time out of 60 minutes of total session time. Is there any comment on the agenda? Okay, so if there is no comment, then let's proceed to the usual report on working group documents status. Since the last ATF, we have two new RFCs. Those are RFC 9926 on prefix registration for IPv6 naval discovery and RFC 9927 on fixing the C flag in the extended address. And then, we have the second RFC,   which is the address registration option, EARO. Congratulations to the authors. Pascal is an author in both documents. And then, in the second one, there's also Adnan. Congratulations and thanks to both for the efforts. And also, thanks a lot to everyone who has contributed with reviews and comments in the process. By the way, the second RFC, you may recall that the initial version was created less than one year ago. This is a very short document, and possibly, this gives us something like the lower bound of the time it seems like a document can take to go through the whole process until publication. So again, thanks everyone who has contributed for this. And we currently have five working group documents. The first two are quite advanced in the process as publication has been requested for these first two drafts, which are again PASA, which stands for Path of Ware, semantic addressing for LLNs, and GAU, which is generic address assignment option for 6.0.1 MD. And then, the other documents are all of the same.   So, we are a little bit behind in different stages. There would be the transmission of sheet compressed package over 15.4 networks, which has been updated and will be presented later today. Also, transmission of IPv6 package over short-range optical wireless communications has been updated and will be presented. And finally, you may recall that in December, we have a new working group document adopted, which is the transmission of IPv6 over multi-drop serial bus token passing MSTP networks, which is intended to obsolete RFC 8163. And it will also be presented later today. So, is there any comment, any question on the status report? Yeah, Eric? Yeah, so very quickly for the first two drafts, the PASA and the GAU, it's there currently on my to-do list, as I need to do the ADA review. But as I agree with Luigi, we have other things to do this week and during the ADA review. So, I expect the ADA review end of next week, and then we can progress the ITF last goal and so on. So, good progress. Happy to see those two documents moving forward. Thank you. Okay, thank you very much. Okay, any other comment or question? Otherwise, I guess we can proceed to the next presentation, which will be by Luigi on the status of PASA and GAU. Yeah, so that will be relatively quick. So, as Eric said, the publication of the two documents has been requested. Eric, at some point, will do the review, as he usually does in a very thorough way, which I appreciate. There was no real update actually on the PASA document, because as you recall, PASA is just an application of GAU. In GAU, we have an update date. I will present a couple of minutes, but no technical changes. So, we didn't have to submit a new document. We just did a revision of this document, which is pretty stable. So, thanks to everybody that gave inputs to this. And I want to say thanks, Eric, for taking care from now on. And that's it for this document. Unless there are comments, I just changed the slide deck. So, for this one, for GAU, there was actually an update. As you know, rightfully, the chair asked for feedback from Sixpan. Okay. So, Lorenzo was quite nice in sending an email, stating his concerns. Okay. And we took a bit of time to address them, but finally we did it. Actually, kudos to Adnan that managed this. So, technically, there is no change from a really technical point of view. But we revised it completely, basically the introduction, so to clarify the scope of the project. So, it's a small option, in which case it might be useful, in which case it might not be so useful. Okay. So, basically, what we added is we discussed in which cases the DHCPv6 in the context of LLNs, it may be not that efficient as it usually is. Mainly because of broadcast, the centralized architecture, okay, and which leads to having to deal with multiple paths. As you know, in Sixpan, we try to avoid this so that we manage everything without broadcast and in a single hop. Okay. And also, we clarified the algorithmic address assignment. Okay. Trying to really clarify in a clear way the benefits of Gao. So, this option is a generic option that allows you to deploy any algorithm that allows you to allocate addresses. Okay. Pasa is an example. There are a couple of references in the document that suggest other algorithms that can be used, but we don't actually, there are no specifications. Okay. We have only Pasa for now. Everything is one hop. Okay. So, we don't need the multi-hop infrastructure and is completely aligned with 8505, which is the main document of about 6LoND. Okay. We did reply a little bit with some delay to Lorenzo. We hope that all the clarification are good. So far, we didn't have a reply. So, yeah, but open, obviously. And that's it, basically. So, again, this document is as well in the hands of Eric now. And I see Lorenzo is in the queue. So, I'm going to try to get the response.   Lorenzo, you can ask the question or the comment. Go ahead. Yeah. So, I'm not super convinced about the rationale here, right? Because I think the discussion statement and that was added to the draft kind of boils down to, you know, the ECP with a central server is kind of expensive, right? That was one big reason why the draft states that the ECPv6 can't be used. And I think the reason for that is that the, in this current proposal, the prefix can be assigned locally, right? That's a big reason for that is that it can be assigned by the neighboring router, right? But, like, there's no reason why, right? There's no reason why you can't use the HPv6 to that neighboring router and, like, cut out all of the multicast noise and all of the expensive things that section, you know, I think just maybe to clarify what I'm saying, it's like, you know, section 101, right? Basically says, well, you know, the ECP is very expensive because you end up kind of flooding the network, which ends up being, you know, you have duty cycles and, you know, so on and so forth. But there's no reason to do that. You can, you're defining a new protocol here, which is a one-hop protocol. You could just as well use the HPv6 as your one-hop protocol. So there are two things that I may reply. First of all, we don't say that you don't have to, you can't use the HPv6. We never use that. We just claim that it might be less efficient, okay? And I think that the discussion is, a little bit asymmetric in the sense that this GAO option is just an option in a bigger framework, okay? That already replaces the DHCPv6. So NSP has proven to be more efficient compared to DHCPv6 in the way it is deployed right now. So it's not a matter of GAO. It's more about the neighbor discovery, how it's done today. Now, if you tell me you can trick DHCPv6, tweak DHCPv6 to have an equivalent efficiency like the framework that we have today in six-level parallel, maybe, I don't know, maybe, but please go ahead, write a draft and state how you can tweak DHCPv6. There's no issue with that. It's not difficult, right? DHCPv6 is a one-packet exchange. Agree, it's not difficult. I'm not claiming it's difficult. My point is, the bar to introducing a new protocol, the point I was trying to make, which is how we got to this text, was that we are adding a new protocol to do something that we already have an existing protocol for. No, that's not accurate. We are not adding a new protocol. We are adding the GAO as an option to an existing protocol, if you wish. Sorry, my bad. It's just a variant of the NGC. My bad. Mechanism. Mechanism. If you want to call it a protocol, you can say mechanism. We are adding a new configuration mechanism to sort of assign prefixes when we already have a configuration mechanism to assign prefixes that has been with us. It's stable. It's deployed everywhere. Anyway, so I'm not going to repeat my previous email, but the point was we have duplication of, we have duplication of, we have duplication of mechanisms, and that is generally not a good thing. Now, the answer to that from the draft author's point of view is like, well, the protocol that, sorry, the mechanism that we have that is used elsewhere, GCPv6, is inefficient in LNs. And what I'm pointing out here is that that's actually not true. It is a one-packet exchange. The GCPv6 server does not need to be removed. You can just run it on the, in the same place that responds to the ERO. Because at the end of the day, any mechanism that we define, including this one, will have one packet that goes to some entity that decides what the prefix is, and one reply that tells the requesting node, here is the prefix that you asked for. And this is what GCPv6 does. You have a client and you have a server. Yes, you can also have a relay. Yes, you can also do multicast. But there's nothing preventing you from just running this two-packet exchange on whatever network today. And GCPv6 servers are already widely available. There's probably like tiny ones that are like very cheap to implement. So anyway, and the refresh timers, you know, can be changed. Anyway, I'm not going to repeat my previous discussion in terms of efficiency, but I think if we want to basically say, you know, we are going to come up with a different mechanism, we need to justify it a little bit better because the justification that's there is not convincing. For you. Okay. No, no, no. I'm giving you a concrete technical concern. The text here assumes that the server must be remote and that this is going to be expensive. And that's not true. You can run the server in the same place as the, I don't know, I'm not going to, I don't know what term to use, but whatever it is that assigns the prefix in this scheme, maybe the neighboring VR, that thing could just as well run the GCPv6 server. And the justification in section 1.1 would not be true anymore. Can you prove that the GCPv6 is as efficient as the Gao option? It's the same thing. It's both ways, Lorenzo, in a certain way. But I don't want to repeat myself again. So this is not a protocol, it's an option. It's not a duplicate because it's a layer, it's not exactly the same use case. If you can prove that the GCPv6 is efficient as Gao, that's fine with me. I have no problems. The burden should be on the team. So, Eric, here. Lorenzo, Luigi, we are in an IETF meeting. I understand both of you are passionate about what you are doing. And you are coming from a country which is known for having exuberant language, which is quiet and polite. So let's keep technical. What we do here is interoperability documents is not required to prove anything to be better or worse than anything else. There is a discussion that is a different use case, is a small option and the use case is actually not GAUD. GAUD is a little piece of the puzzle, it is a bigger one. So I don't have anything to add to that. The different mechanisms to allocate addresses and prefixes, something that exists even before GAUD. I was sitting here listening to this discussion, getting a bit contentious and I think there's miscommunication going on. I think the productive way to bring things to the ITF is to say there is this thing that we want to do when it goes into and we have to do it this way, is when the conversation gets stuck. I agree with what Lorenzo said, is that when there is already a perfectly good way of doing this, then the presumption is let's reuse existing mechanisms. I think there was some miscommunication in the way of talking about the protocol of the network. We use the term protocol in networking when the same way it is used in diplomacy about protocol and diplomacy is who does what, when and who speaks first and who replies. For protocol is a set of rules for how things work. If we're adding it we can call it a mechanism. If we're adding a new way of doing something, that is a new set of rules for what entities what send messages, what entities receive messages, and reply to them. The burden on bringing proposals to the ITF is to convince other people that we need a new thing. The burden is not on the ITF to to come up with the counter argument. I think to move this discussion forward we either say yeah Lorenzo makes a good point, we can actually do this with existing mechanisms, or we come up with a really strong argument why we have to do this. We can do something new. So that's my suggestion. That's a very good point. Thank you. Just for clarification, we never say that we have to replace anything. We never said you can't use existing mechanisms. So just an alternative in a specific very scoped use case. Oh this is not about the replacing things. This reminds me of a comment that John Postel made 15 years ago. He said the early work of the ITF was to look at all of the many possible ways of doing something and to agree on the one way we're going to do it. And in recent years the work of the ITF has been filling in all the other ways of doing the same thing that we said no to the first time around. And it's a very good point. I think it's a very good point. I think it's a very good point.  And having two ways of doing something is not a benefit. It's a downside. Our job here is to look at the menu of choices and pick the one we're going to do, not to say yes to all of them. I just wanted to ask a sort of process question. Sorry, I should probably know the answer already. But I feel that this is a sort of legitimate technical concern in terms of duplication of protocols, right? I just want to know where to send that. Can I just send it to the working group mailing list or, you know, because this was part of the six-man review. And yes, I apologize for not replying, but oops if you want to answer. Yeah, yeah. Yeah, I also have concerns about duplication and fragmentation of the protocol and so on. So I think I'd like to talk to Bob about this and we might talk to chairs as well. And now we have the same responsible AD for this, right? So I guess it might be, yeah, a bit of administrative discussion. But yeah, but with no hats on, I do see some concerns here about creating 15th competing standards here, right? So maybe, yeah, I apologize. We probably need to pin the six-man again. Yeah, and I'll do something to bring it to their attention. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.      Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. And if there are, if the justification isn't sufficient, we can probably build on top of that. Indeed, thank you. Okay, thank you. So now we move to the next presentation. In this case, I will present the last update of the draft entitled transmission of sheet, compressed packets over IEEE 802.15.4 networks. My coauthor is Ana Minaburo. And first of all, a quick reminder on the main goal in this draft. On the left, you can see on the left in this slide, the traditional IPv6 based protocol stack for devices that use 15.4 as the radio interface. This is possible thanks to six low-panel and adaptation layer, which from some point of view could be seen as comprising, you know, the IPv6 protocol stack.        So we can see that there are two sub layers on top, six low-panel header compression and below six low-panel fragmentation. On the right of this slide, we can see the protocol stack that we want to enable, which is basically offering using Sheik as an alternative technique for header compression in these environments. And that's because there's the expectation that Sheik might offer greater header compression performance. Sheik is the main product of the LP1 working group, now Sheik working group, and Sheik exploits a priori knowledge of the header field values of the packets that will be transmitted. Then on the status of the draft, it was adopted three years ago. And today I'm presenting version 12, which incorporates several improvements after Esco Dyck's comprehensive and thoughtful review. Many thanks Esco. And the improvements are in several areas, including technical, editorial, and also presentational format. And we also align some terms in this document with the part of the new terminology from the Sheik architecture draft, which appears to be more stable as well. On the table of contents, the main structure remains stable. There is no new section. So structure remains the same as in the previous version of the draft. And now let's go through the updates in this last revision. First of all, in section 2.2, we had some text about the six low-pan node term 6LN. This was defined in RFC 6775 as any host or router participating in a low-pan. However, then there is another document, RFC 9008, which describes how Ripple can be used in a number of scenarios. This is a very common scenario that constrains the definition of 6LN to only the role of leaf node. And in this draft, we had adopted the same approach because of the text that we were using for the Ripple related content. You may recall that for route over, well for multi-hop communication, we have three route of modes. One of them is the tile-based route over. And however, we realized after a command from Esco, that the definition of 6LN in 9008 is too constraining, considering that node might act sometimes as a router, but in some cases, perhaps not, and considering the whole document. So what we did was removing the paragraph in 2.2 that was constraining the use of 6LN to just the leaf node. Then we have some updates in the introductory part of section 3.5, on multi-hop communication. So first of all, Esco explained that there exist solutions in commercial networks that combine features from both route over and mesh under. You may recall that in 3.5 for multi-hop communication, we define again, few modes for route over and then mesh under. And what we have explained is that such solutions that are hybrid, use the functionality defined in this document for multi-hop communication as appropriate. Then you may also recall that in this section, there is a number of figures illustrating some example scenarios, and we have some clarifications on those. One of them is that there are rules shown only, meaning rules for compression decompression CD, shown only for IPv6 or IPv6 plus upper layers jointly, compression decompression. And additional six strata can also be used, not only one as is currently shown in the figures. But of course, if there are more strata, then the corresponding rules will need to be stored as well. And the clarification is that the other strata and the other rules for the other strata are not shown in the figures for the sake of clarity. And on the other hand, the other rules for the other strata are not shown in the figures for the sake of clarity. And on the other hand, similar to what I was mentioning before, routers in the figures do not generate application layer messages in these examples. And however, in real scenarios, nodes acting as routers might at some time also act as hosts, for example, generating application layer messages. And again, this is not shown in the figures for the sake of clarity, but it doesn't mean it cannot happen. And we also add that such nodes will need to support also the functionality for hosts. And also we added here, again in the introduction part of 3.5, that routers may use Schick CD also for the transmission of control plane or management plane messages. They will need to store the corresponding rules, and they will need to use single-hop or multi-hop transmission procedures accordingly. One comment here also added to the draft is that, okay, Schick can be used as long as the host is running. And there is some specification that defines how it can be used to compress, decompress the header of some particular protocol. So far, on top of IPv6, Schick CD has been defined for UDP, CoAP, SMPv6, and there's also some initial work for QUIC. Then we have also some updates in section 4.3 on PRO, which is the pointer-based route over mode. Okay, about the destination context identifier, which is something you can use optionally. The idea is that, as a suggestion by Esco as well, if there is a network that comprises nodes that only use Schick for CD and nodes that use only traditional six-loop-band CD, and if we are using destination context identifier, then the prefix context to be used for both types of nodes should be the same for simplicity. And also, Esco had a request or at least pointed out that, in some cases, such as in thread networks, routers are expected to have access to ECN bits to signal congestion, for instance. So what we did was modify a little bit the definition of the bit pointer. So that PRO can also be used in a way that routers can access those ECN bits. And the new definition is written on the slide. The bit pointer gives the starting position of traffic class, followed by the hop limit and the IPv6 destination address in the Schick residue of the Schick-compressed IPv6 header index. So the new part is what is highlighted in red. And with this, and now there are, out of the three routable modes, there are two which support ECN-capable routers. The straightforward route over, where routers have all the rules in use, and also pointer-based route over, where the routers don't have the rules used by other endpoints. But with this modification, both modes can support ECN-capable operation. Okay, and still in the same section, we have renamed the field called address length to address residue length, as it indicates the size of the IPv6 destination address residue in bits. And a scope pointed out that, yeah, the possible values encoded by this field range from 0 to 127, which would not be enough to represent from 0 to 128. And tentatively and currently, we have, uh, text saying that value 127, uh, would be used when the IPv6 destination address residue size is either 127 or 128 bits. And this, uh, could work well for, uh, the final endpoint and for all routers, except for the last one, where, uh, this last one might have to have access to the full destination address to be able to, uh, deliver the, the packet to the final destination. And,  here, uh, this is still not fully solved. We may need to, to consider how to fix this. Um, for example, in internet work communication, or if, uh, we are using the DCI, the context that identifies a set of, uh, destination prefixes. So this is something that we need to, uh, sort out. And if you have thoughts on this, uh, please let us know.
